holding: Installing/using a pen register to record dialed phone numbers is not a search; a person has no reasonable expectation of privacy in information voluntarily conveyed to a third party (the phone company). Origin/articulation of the third-party doctrine.
quote: This Court consistently has held that a person has no legitimate expectation of privacy in information he voluntarily turns over to third parties.
point_cited_for: Third-party doctrine origin; pen register not a search

holding: Bank records (checks, deposit slips) are the bank's business records, not the customer's private papers; no reasonable expectation of privacy in information voluntarily conveyed to the bank and exposed to its employees. Foundational third-party-doctrine case (pre-Smith).
point_cited_for: No REP in records shared with a third party (bank records)

holding: Government acquisition of historical cell-site location information (CSLI) is a Fourth Amendment search requiring a warrant; the third-party doctrine of Smith/Miller does NOT extend to the comprehensive, automatically-generated record of physical movements that CSLI provides. A narrow decision (does not disturb conventional surveillance or other business records).
quote: These location records 'hold for many Americans the "privacies of life."'
point_cited_for: CSLI not covered by third-party doctrine; warrant required for digital location records
correction: POST-2015 cluster/opinion gotcha: cluster id 4510032 (canonical link) resolves to readable lead opinion id 4287285 (Roberts, 2018-06-22). Confirmed case name 'Carpenter v. United States' in opinion text. (Cluster 4512666 / opinion 4289919 dated 2018-06-29 is a redline-diff duplicate - do not use.)

holding: Using a thermal imager (sense-enhancing technology not in general public use) to detect details of a home's interior that would otherwise require physical intrusion is a search, presumptively unreasonable without a warrant.
quote: Where, as here, the Government uses a device that is not in general public use, to explore details of the home that would previously have been unknowable without physical intrusion, the surveillance is a 'search' and is presumptively unreasonable without a warrant.
point_cited_for: Sense-enhancing tech not in general public use -> search (home interior)

cite: United States v. Hay, 95 F.4th 1304 (10th Cir. 2024)
court: U.S. Court of Appeals, Tenth Circuit
holding: Continuous pole-camera surveillance of the exterior of a home (here, ~over two months) capturing only what was visible to passersby from public vantage did not violate the Fourth Amendment; the court rejected the argument that Carpenter abrogated its prior pole-camera precedent (United States v. Jackson).
point_cited_for: Pole cameras; public-view exterior surveillance not a search post-Carpenter (in the 10th Cir.)
correction: FLAG CIRCUIT SPLIT: pole-camera law is unsettled. The 1st Cir. en banc (United States v. Moore-Bush, 36 F.4th 320 (1st Cir. 2022)) split evenly, and Massachusetts (Commonwealth v. Mora) and other courts have found long-term pole-camera surveillance can be a search. Present Hay as one circuit's view, not a settled federal rule.

cite: United States v. Porter, No. 25-60163 (5th Cir. Mar. 17, 2026) [reporter cite not yet assigned]
court: U.S. Court of Appeals, Fifth Circuit
holding: Use of an automatic license plate reader (ALPR/LPR) to obtain vehicle-location data and generate a hit (outstanding warrant) did not constitute a Fourth Amendment search; the ALPR hit gave reasonable suspicion to support the traffic stop. (Also rejected a Second Amendment challenge to 18 U.S.C. 922(o).)
quote: the use of an LPR did not constitute a search
point_cited_for: ALPR vehicle-location data not a search; ALPR hit supports reasonable suspicion
correction: WRONG-CITE (reporter): manifest cite '170 F.4th 381 (2026)' could NOT be confirmed - no F.4th reporter cite is assigned to this opinion in CourtListener yet (decided 2026-03-17, too recent). The CASE is real and verified by docket No. 25-60163 / opinion text. Cite by docket until the F.4th cite issues; do NOT assert '170 F.4th 381' as confirmed.

cite: Robinson v. Commonwealth, ___ Va. App. ___ (2026) [Eddie Eugene Robinson; reporter cite not yet assigned]
holding: Use of a network of Flock automatic license plate reader cameras was not a Fourth Amendment search; the defendant had no reasonable expectation of privacy in his vehicle's physical characteristics/movements on public streets (analogizing to United States v. Knotts), and Norfolk's 172-camera system did not amount to the 'near perfect surveillance' that triggered a warrant requirement in Carpenter / Leaders of a Beautiful Struggle.
quote: Robinson had no reasonable expectation of privacy in the physical characteristics of his vehicle as he drove it down a public street.
point_cited_for: ALPR/Flock camera network not a search (state-illustrative); distinguishes Carpenter on scale
correction: WRONG-CITE: manifest cite '927 S.E.2d 822 (Va. Ct. App. ~2024)' does NOT resolve - no such S.E.2d citation matches this case (the published opinion is dated 2026-04-07 and has no S.E.2d cite assigned yet). The CASE is real (Eddie Eugene Robinson v. Commonwealth, Va. Ct. App., published, 2026-04-07) and is the ALPR/Flock case described. Do NOT assert '927 S.E.2d 822' or a 2024 date; cite as a 2026 Va. Ct. App. published opinion pending reporter cite.

holding: Warrantless naked-eye aerial observation of a fenced backyard from navigable airspace (~1,000 ft) is not a search - the curtilage was knowingly exposed to any aircraft lawfully in public airspace. Anchor of the aerial-surveillance lineage relevant to drones.
point_cited_for: Aerial-surveillance lineage (drones); lawful-vantage observation not a search

holding: Warrantless naked-eye observation from a helicopter at 400 feet into a partially covered greenhouse within the curtilage was not a search (plurality), where the aircraft was in lawful, non-rare public airspace. Closest SCOTUS analogue for low-altitude drone surveillance.
point_cited_for: Aerial-surveillance lineage (drones); low-altitude helicopter observation not a search

case: Dow Chemical Co. v. United States
cite: Dow Chemical Co. v. United States, 476 U.S. 227 (1986)
holding: Warrantless EPA aerial photography of an industrial complex from navigable airspace, using a standard commercial mapping camera, was not a search; sense-enhancement by widely available technology over open commercial areas did not cross the line (contrast Kyllo's 'not in general public use').
point_cited_for: Aerial photography of commercial premises not a search; ties aerial lineage to the general-public-use technology line
